text: There were two angels according to St Luke (24.4); perhaps one of them was seen inside the tomb, the other outside (Mt. 28.2), and the women may not have been agreed, whether it was the same angel they saw in both places.
‘Peter and the rest of his disciples’; literally, ‘his disciples and Peter’, cf. Ac. 1.14.

vv. 1-8: Mt. 28.1; Lk. 24.1. v. 8: That is, evidently, they said nothing to those whom they met on their way; if St Mary Magdalen went on in front of the others, they will have passed St Peter and St John (Jn. 20.3).

vv. 9-20: It seems that the manuscripts of St Mark were mutilated at the end in very early times; the whole of this chapter being sometimes omitted (St Jerome Ad Hedyb. q. 3). And in a few of our existing manuscripts these last twelve verses are wanting, which fact (together with the abruptness of their style) has made some critics think that they were added from another source. But they are evidently a primitive account, and there is no reason why we should not ascribe their inclusion here to St Mark.
